description_long: This object is a tall, monolithic guard stone featuring a prominent Celtic cross at its top, with a circular ring connecting the cross arms. The shaft is rectangular and elongated, adorned with intricate, low-relief spiral and knotwork patterns typical of historical stonework. The surface appears weathered and rough, with visible textural irregularities and signs of age. The cross section is relatively narrow, and the object is solid and heavy, likely carved from a single block of stone. The coloration is a mix of gray and greenish hues, suggesting exposure to the elements and possible moss or lichen growth. This type of guard stone would serve both as a protective barrier and a decorative or commemorative marker in an urban or historical setting.
description: A tall, weathered stone guard stone with a Celtic cross top and intricate spiral carvings, showing signs of age and outdoor exposure.
description_view_0: Front view shows the full Celtic cross head and detailed spiral carvings along the shaft.
description_view_1: Side view reveals the narrow profile and thickness of the stone, with minimal visible decoration.
description_view_2: Rear view displays the back of the cross and shaft, with less pronounced carvings and a more uniform surface.
description_view_3: Opposite side view highlights the slimness and rough texture of the stone, similar to view 1.
attribute_location: Typically found at the entrance to historical sites, along urban street corners, or adjacent to pathways in parks, often marking boundaries or protecting structures from vehicle encroachment.
attribute_installation_orientation: Meant to be installed fully above ground, standing upright and often partially embedded at the base for stability.
attribute_wear_patterns: Visible surface erosion, chipping along edges, and discoloration from weathering and possible vehicle contact; moss or lichen growth in recessed areas, with the base and cross arms most affected by impact and environmental exposure.
attribute_historical_markings: Intricate spiral and knotwork carvings on the front shaft and cross, typical of Celtic or early medieval stonework; no visible inscriptions or text.
